skill.name: python-performance-optimization
skill.description: Profile and optimize Python code with CPU profiling, memory profiling, benchmarking, async I/O, database optimization, and practical performance patterns.
skill.summary: Guides Python profiling and optimization with practical examples for CPU, memory, I/O, database, and benchmarking work.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows the files are Markdown guidance and examples. The real low-risk concerns are user-run profiling commands and a public HTTP benchmark example; no malicious intent, credential access, or prompt injection evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown examples misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts mostly point to Markdown code fences and inline file references, not Ruby backtick execution. The skill contains documentation examples and does not automatically execute these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is inside Markdown examples or inline resource paths. I found no code path where the skill executes Ruby backticks or shell commands automatically.

low_findings.title: User-run profiling commands require local process access
low_findings.description: The playbook recommends commands such as cProfile, kernprof, memory_profiler, py-spy, and pytest-benchmark. These are legitimate profiling tools, but py-spy examples inspect a running process by PID and should be run only on authorized systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and can inspect local Python processes. Risk is low because the skill is documentation-only and the examples are standard profiling workflows.

low_findings.title: Public HTTP benchmark endpoint in async I/O example
low_findings.description: The async I/O section uses repeated requests to https://httpbin.org/delay/1 to compare synchronous and asynchronous requests. This creates outbound traffic if copied and run, but it does not send secrets or user data in the documented example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and hardcoded URL are explicit in the example. I found no evidence that credentials, files, or environment variables are transmitted.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections point to ordinary descriptive text and a line_profiler decorator comment, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain performance guidance text and an @profile decorator comment. There is no cryptographic algorithm or security-sensitive hashing operation on these lines.

low_findings.title: SQLite database example is local and non-sensitive
low_findings.description: The SQLite alert points to a local example database used for query planning demonstration. The example does not access real credentials, production data, or external storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SQLite usage is a teaching example around EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN. The only database path shown is example.db, and no sensitive data source is referenced.

low_findings.title: Critical combination heuristic not confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined documentation examples for local commands, network benchmarking, and other benign snippets. I found no credential access, obfuscation, exfiltration logic, or prompt injection attempt in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are visible, but semantic review shows they are unrelated tutorial snippets. No evidence found for credential access or malicious data flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authorized local profiler command examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that profile scripts or inspect a local PID. These commands are acceptable for a Python performance skill when used with authorization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The py-spy examples are real local inspection commands. They are standard profiling usage and do not indicate malicious automation by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound benchmark requests in documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The async I/O benchmark includes requests to httpbin.org. This is a low-risk public demo endpoint, but users should replace it with approved internal targets when needed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL and request calls are directly present. The example sends only requests to a public delay endpoint and does not include secret material.

content.value_statement: Slow Python services and data jobs are hard to fix without evidence. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through profiling, benchmarking, and targeted optimization steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use CPU, memory, line, and production profilers.
Shows cProfile, pstats, line_profiler, memory_profiler, py-spy, and pytest-benchmark workflows.
Recommends optimization patterns for loops, generators, strings, dictionaries, caching, and NumPy.
Covers multiprocessing for CPU-bound work and async I/O for I/O-bound work.
Includes database, memory leak, iterator, weak reference, and benchmarking examples.
Provides checklists for measuring before and after performance changes.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not automatically profile or modify a project.
Examples may need package installation before they run locally.
Benchmark results depend on hardware, data size, Python version, and workload shape.
It does not replace production observability, load testing, or security review.

use_cases.title: Find service bottlenecks
use_cases.description: Use profiler output to identify slow functions and focus changes on hot paths.

use_cases.title: Reduce memory usage
use_cases.description: Compare lists, generators, iterators, weak references, and memory snapshots for large workloads.

use_cases.title: Improve database and I/O throughput
use_cases.description: Apply batching, indexes, async I/O, and benchmark comparisons to reduce latency.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a profiler
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage for slow Python code
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the right Python profiler for this performance issue. Ask for the workload type, runtime context, and symptoms before recommending steps.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze profiler output
prompt_templates.scenario: Interpreting cProfile or memory profiler results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this profiler output and identify the top bottlenecks. Explain which measurements matter and recommend the first three safe changes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an optimization pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python optimization plan for this module. Include baseline metrics, candidate changes, expected tradeoffs, and verification benchmarks.

prompt_templates.title: Design production profiling
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced profiling on a live service
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production-safe profiling workflow for this Python service. Include authorization checks, sampling tools, rollback criteria, and evidence to collect.

output_examples.input: A web endpoint has high latency, and cProfile shows most time in serialization and database calls.
output_examples.output: A ranked bottleneck summary, recommended database batching and indexing checks, serializer improvements, and a before-and-after benchmark plan.

output_examples.input: A data processing job uses too much memory when reading large files.
output_examples.output: A memory-focused diagnosis that compares full-list loading with iterator processing, adds measurement steps, and lists expected memory savings.

output_examples.input: A CPU-heavy calculation runs slowly during batch processing.
output_examples.output: A plan to benchmark algorithm changes, evaluate NumPy or multiprocessing, and confirm speedup without changing output correctness.

best_practices: Profile before optimizing and keep baseline measurements for comparison.
Change one bottleneck at a time and verify speed, memory, and correctness after each change.
Run profiling commands only on systems and processes you are authorized to inspect.
anti_patterns: Do not optimize code paths that are not measured as meaningful bottlenecks.
Do not use public benchmark URLs in restricted or sensitive environments.
Do not assume microbenchmark wins will improve full application performance.

faq.question: Does this skill run profilers automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that a user can apply in an authorized environment.

faq.question: Which profilers does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers cProfile, pstats, line_profiler, memory_profiler, py-spy, tracemalloc, and pytest-benchmark.

faq.question: Can it help with memory leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes tracemalloc, garbage collection, iterator, and weak reference patterns for memory analysis.

faq.question: Can it optimize database queries?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for batching writes, adding indexes, and reviewing query plans.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Use production profiling only with authorization, low-overhead tools, and a rollback plan.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
